response: The EU’s General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), effective May 2018, applies globally to any firm handling EU residents’ data and grants rights such as access, erasure, portability, and explicit consent, while mandating Data Protection Officers for large‑scale processing and 72‑hour breach notifications. Violations can incur fines up to 4 % of global annual revenue or €20 million, with notable penalties including a €1.2 billion fine against Meta in 2023, €746 million for Amazon, and over €200 million for GDPR‑related fines on Google. Though GDPR has inspired similar privacy laws worldwide (e.g., CCPA, LGPD, India’s DPDPA), critics say it imposes heavy compliance costs—especially on small businesses—and leads to user fatigue from cookie banners, while enforcement varies across member states. Nonetheless, the regulation has cemented data privacy as a fundamental right and set the de‑facto global standard for data protection.
